fragments.content: Lipid disorders encompass abnormalities in cholesterol and triglyceride metabolism that increase atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) risk. Major types: (1) Elevated LDL cholesterol - primary driver of atherosclerosis; (2) Low HDL cholesterol - associated with increased CVD risk though raising HDL hasn't shown benefit; (3) Hypertriglyceridemia - independent risk factor, severe levels (>500 mg/dL) cause pancreatitis; (4) Elevated lipoprotein(a) - genetically determined, independent CVD risk factor. Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH): autosomal dominant disorder (LDLR, APOB, PCSK9 mutations) causing very high LDL from birth (heterozygous LDL 190-450 mg/dL, homozygous >450 mg/dL), premature ASCVD, tendon xanthomas, corneal arcus. Prevalence 1:200-250 for heterozygous FH. Secondary causes: hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome, cholestatic liver disease, medications (thiazides, beta-blockers, retinoids, immunosuppressants, antiretrovirals).

Treatment based on ASCVD risk stratification. Statins first-line for LDL lowering: high-intensity (atorvastatin 40-80mg, rosuvastatin 20-40mg) for clinical ASCVD or high-risk primary prevention; moderate-intensity for intermediate risk. Add ezetimibe if LDL remains above threshold on maximally tolerated statin. PCSK9 inhibitors (alirocumab, evolocumab) or inclisiran (siRNA) for FH or statin-intolerant patients not at goal. Bempedoic acid alternative for statin-intolerant. For severe hypertriglyceridemia (≥500 mg/dL): fibrates (fenofibrate preferred over gemfibrozil with statins), prescription omega-3 fatty acids (icosapent ethyl - REDUCE-IT trial showed CVD benefit; omega-3 acid ethyl esters), niacin rarely used now. Lifestyle foundation: Mediterranean diet, reduce saturated/trans fats, increase soluble fiber, weight loss, regular exercise, limit alcohol (raises triglycerides). FH requires aggressive early treatment, often combination therapy, cascade screening of relatives.

Diagnosis via fasting or non-fasting lipid panel: total cholesterol, HDL-C, triglycerides, calculated or direct LDL-C. Non-HDL cholesterol (total - HDL) increasingly used as it captures all atherogenic particles. Apolipoprotein B (apoB) reflects particle number, may be superior predictor. Lipoprotein(a) measured once in lifetime to identify genetic elevation (>50 mg/dL or >125 nmol/L). Screening: adults 20+ every 4-6 years if low risk; more frequently with risk factors. Children: universal screening once at 9-11 years and again at 17-21; targeted screening earlier if family history. FH diagnosis: Dutch Lipid Clinic Network criteria use LDL level, physical findings (xanthomas, corneal arcus), family history of premature ASCVD/high cholesterol, genetic testing if available. Evaluate secondary causes: TSH, liver enzymes, creatinine, urine protein, glucose/HbA1c, medication review. Risk calculators (ASCVD Risk Estimator, SCORE) guide treatment decisions.
